note.soap:
S: Patient reports a tick bite with a bullseye rash present. Tick was attached for approximately 37 hours. No systemic symptoms reported. Exposure occurred in Georgia. Pregnancy not possible.

O: Unable to perform physical exam due to phone-only encounter. Patient describes characteristic erythema migrans rash (bullseye).

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease  
  - Differential:  
    1. Early Lyme disease  
    2. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
    3. Cellulitis or other tick-bite related skin infection  
    4. Allergic reaction to tick bite  
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, joint pain, neurological symptoms, or cardiorespiratory complaints reported.

P:  
  - Disposition: Routine follow-up with primary care or infectious disease as needed  
  - Plan: Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy for early Lyme disease per CDC and IDSA guidelines (e.g., doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-21 days), educate patient on signs of progression or systemic involvement, and advise on tick prevention measures.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care if fever, worsening rash, joint swelling, neurological symptoms (e.g., facial palsy, headache), or cardiac symptoms develop.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- CDC guidance recommends treatment of early Lyme disease with characteristic erythema migrans rash and tick exposure in endemic areas.  
- AAFP review supports doxycycline as first-line therapy for early localized Lyme disease.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
